title: Competing Effects of Cohesive Energy and Cross-Link Density on the Segmental Dynamics and Mechanical Properties of Cross-Linked Polymers
abstract: To develop structure—property relationships for cross-linked thermosetting polymers, it is crucial to better understand key factors that control their segmental dynamics and macroscopic properties. Here, we employ a coarse-grained (CG) polymer model to systematically explore the combined effect of varying the cohesive energy  $(\varepsilon)$  and cross-link density (c) on the segmental relaxation time and mechanical properties for a model cross-linked glass-forming thermoset material. We find that increasing c increases both the glass transition temperature  $T_g$  and fragility of glass formation, while the fragility decreases with an increase in  $\varepsilon$ . These competing effects of  $\varepsilon$  and c on fragility are practically important since fragility determines the overall temperature width of the glass formation over which the non-Arrhenius temperature dependence is observed. Our simulation results show that the basic mechanical properties (*i.e.*, bulk and shear moduli) of cross-linked thermosets are mainly influenced by  $\varepsilon$ . More interestingly, the macroscopic mechanical properties are found to be strongly correlated with the Debye–Waller parameter  $\langle u^2 \rangle$ , a measure of material "stiffness" at a molecular level. In particular, the distribution of local molecular stiffness,  $1/\langle u^2 \rangle$ , exhibits a nearly universal Gaussian distribution at a fixed reduced temperature  $T/T_g$ . Our work reveals the key and competitive roles of cohesive energy and cross-link density in controlling the segmental dynamics, large scale, and local mechanical properties of cross-linked thermosets, providing an understanding that should be useful in the molecular design of these materials.
results: **3.1. Thermodynamic Properties.** We first analyze the influence of c and  $\varepsilon$  on the thermodynamic properties of crosslinked thermosets, including density  $\rho$ , thermal expansion coefficient  $\alpha_{\rm p}$ , and isothermal compressibility  $\kappa_{\rm T}$ , which are fundamental quantities to characterize the thermodynamics. These thermodynamic properties as a function of T are summarized in Figure S1 in the Supporting Information. As expected, the increase in  $\varepsilon$  and c leads to an obvious increase in  $\rho$  and a significant decrease in  $\alpha_{\rm p}$  and  $\kappa_{\rm T}$  above  $T_{\rm g}$  S7,58 Similar trends have been observed in polymer mixtures, where the density at  $T_{\rm g}$  increased and the isothermal compressibility decreased as the cohesive energy of additives increased. It is notable that the cohesive energy parameter  $\varepsilon$  exhibits a significantly larger influence on the thermodynamic properties than the cross-link density c.
conclusion: In the present work, we employ a bead-spring CG model to systematically explore the combined effect of cross-link density and cohesive energy on the dynamics and mechanical properties for cross-linked thermosets. For cross-linked networks having a fixed cross-link density, the effect of cohesive energy on the thermodynamic properties (i.e., density  $\rho$ , reduced thermal expansion coefficient  $\alpha_p^*$ , and reduced isothermal compressibility  $\kappa_T^*$ ) can be nearly eliminated by a simple shift in the temperature and, thus, a change of the "effective temperature". It is found that the cohesive energy has a more significant influence on the relaxation dynamics than varying the cross-link density. The characteristic temperatures (i.e., the onset temperature  $T_A$ , the glass transition temperature  $T_{\varphi}$ , and the VFT critical temperature  $T_0$ ) increase nearly linearly with increasing cohesive energy. It is notable that the fragility parameters at the same time decrease with increasing cohesive energy strength, while they exhibit an opposite trend with increasing cross-link density. Remarkably, despite the competition between cohesive energy and cross-link density on fragility, the structural relaxation time for all the systems can be described well by the thermodynamic scaling.
